title: Kids' Art Center
summary: A child-height bookshelf and a matching table that cantilevers off it — one weekend build that turns a corner into a craft station.
description:
This is really two small builds that lean on each other: a shallow 1x12 bookshelf and a low table whose far end rests on cleats inside that shelf. That's why the table only needs two legs — the bookshelf carries the other end. It keeps the parts count down and makes the whole thing feel built-in rather than like two pieces of furniture shoved together.

Everything is pocket-hole joinery, so there are no angles and nothing to clamp for an hour. The one thing to get right is that the cleats inside the bookshelf sit at the same height as the tops of the table legs — measure both off the floor, not off each other, or the tabletop will tilt.

Use a pre-finished project panel for the top if you can find one; a kid's art table takes markers, glue, and water, so a durable topcoat matters more here than on most builds. Round every edge and corner well — this is furniture at exactly eye-and-elbow height for a toddler.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs, most of it drying time between coats

tools.note: The whole thing is pocket screws. A jig set for 3/4" stock is the fast path.
A circular saw and a square work fine — these are all square crosscuts.
Handy for tacking shelves while the glue and screws set.
Kids' furniture — every edge gets rounded and every face gets smooth.

materials.name: 1x12 board, 12 ft total
materials.note: Sides and shelves of the bookshelf. Sold as 6- or 8-ft lengths — buy to suit your car.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood or project panel, 48" x 24"
materials.note: A pre-finished birch project panel makes a tougher, easier-to-wipe tabletop.

materials.name: Wood screws, 2"
materials.note: For fastening the table apron to the bookshelf cleats.

cutList.note: Fastened inside the bookshelf to carry one end of the table.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two bookshelf sides at 32" and three shelves at 24" from the 1x12. Cut two bookshelf top at 24" from the 1x3. Cut two bookshelf bottom rails at 24", two table cleats at 11-1/4", two table side aprons at 46-1/2" and one table front apron at 21" from the 1x2. Cut two table legs at 20-1/4" from the 2x2. Cut one tabletop at 48" from the Plywood / project panel. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to one 12 ft 1x12, two 4 ft 1x3, two 8 ft 1x2 and one 4 ft 2x2. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the bookshelf box
steps.body:
Attach the two top rails and two bottom rails to the two 1x12 sides with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws plus glue. If you'd rather not see pocket holes, drive 2" screws through the outside faces of the sides and countersink them.

Check the box for square across both diagonals before the glue grabs — everything downstream references off this box.

steps.title: Install the shelves
steps.body: Set the three 1x12 shelves into the box — the top and bottom shelves land on top of and beneath the rails from the last step, the middle one wherever you want it. Pocket-screw them first, then reinforce by gluing and nailing into the 1x2 and 1x3 rails.

steps.title: Add the table cleats
steps.body: Fasten the two cleats to the inside face of the bookshelf with 1-1/4" screws and glue. Their top edge sets the table height, so measure their position up from the floor — the same number you'll use for the table legs. Get this wrong and the tabletop slopes.

steps.title: Assemble the table frame
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of the front apron and in one end of each side apron. Attach them to the two legs, flush to the inside faces, with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue. Also drill a couple of pocket holes facing up in the aprons now — you'll use them to grab the tabletop.

steps.title: Hang the top and finish
steps.body:
Rest the free end of the table frame on the bookshelf cleats and screw the aprons to the cleats. Set the tabletop on the frame and drive screws up through the apron pocket holes into it.

Sand every edge and corner round — this is a toddler's table. Finish the top with a durable, wipeable topcoat; markers and glue live here now.

images.alt: Child's art center with a low pine bookshelf and an attached craft table
